quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Two-proportion z-test"?

options.text: The initial probability of an event before new evidence is considered; the starting point in Bayesian reasoning
df = k - 1, where k is the number of categories; determines the shape of the chi-square distribution
Tests H₀: p₁ = p₂ using pooled proportion p̂c and z = (p̂₁ - p̂₂)/SE_pooled
Tests H₀ that a categorical variable follows a specified distribution by comparing observed and expected counts

quiz_questions.explanation: "Two-proportion z-test" is defined as: Tests H₀: p₁ = p₂ using pooled proportion p̂c and z = (p̂₁ - p̂₂)/SE_pooled. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Pooled proportion"?

options.text: p̂c = (X₁ + X₂)/(n₁ + n₂); combined success rate across both samples, used in the test statistic under H₀
A ∪ B ("A or B") includes outcomes in either event; A ∩ B ("A and B") includes only outcomes in both events
SD(X ± Y) = √(Var(X) + Var(Y)) for independent random variables; note that SDs do NOT simply add
An unobserved variable that influences both the explanatory and response variables, creating a false impression of a direct relationship

quiz_questions.explanation: "Pooled proportion" is defined as: p̂c = (X₁ + X₂)/(n₁ + n₂); combined success rate across both samples, used in the test statistic under H₀. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "CI for p₁ - p₂"?

options.text: t-procedures are robust to moderate non-normality for large samples but can be misleading for small samples from highly skewed distributions
One-sample t-test applied to the differences: t = d̄/(sd/√n) with df = n-1, testing H₀: μd = 0
(p̂₁ - p̂₂) ± z*·√(p̂₁(1-p̂₁)/n₁ + p̂₂(1-p̂₂)/n₂); uses unpooled standard error since we don't assume p₁ = p₂
One-sided: Hₐ: p > p₀ or p < p₀ (p-value is area in one tail); Two-sided: Hₐ: p ≠ p₀ (p-value is area in both tails)

quiz_questions.explanation: "CI for p₁ - p₂" is defined as: (p̂₁ - p̂₂) ± z*·√(p̂₁(1-p̂₁)/n₁ + p̂₂(1-p̂₂)/n₂); uses unpooled standard error since we don't assume p₁ = p₂. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Large Counts condition (two samples)"?

options.text: The probability of correctly rejecting H₀ when Hₐ is true; Power = 1 - β; increased by larger n, larger effect size, or larger α
The claim we suspect is true and are trying to find evidence for; can be one-sided () or two-sided (≠)
df = k - 1, where k is the number of categories; determines the shape of the chi-square distribution
n₁p̂c ≥ 10, n₁(1-p̂c) ≥ 10, n₂p̂c ≥ 10, and n₂(1-p̂c) ≥ 10 for the z-test

quiz_questions.explanation: "Large Counts condition (two samples)" is defined as: n₁p̂c ≥ 10, n₁(1-p̂c) ≥ 10, n₂p̂c ≥ 10, and n₂(1-p̂c) ≥ 10 for the z-test. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Independent samples"?

options.text: A variable associated with both the explanatory variable and the response variable that distorts the apparent relationship between them
(O - E)/√E for each cell; values beyond ±2 indicate that cell contributes notably to the overall χ² statistic
Occurs when individuals selected for a sample cannot be contacted or refuse to participate, and non-respondents differ systematically from respondents
Observations in one sample must not influence observations in the other; required for valid two-proportion inference

quiz_questions.explanation: "Independent samples" is defined as: Observations in one sample must not influence observations in the other; required for valid two-proportion inference. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "Tests H₀: p₁ = p₂ using pooled proportion p̂c and z = (p̂₁ - p̂₂)/SE_pooled"?

options.text: Retrospective vs. prospective study
Margin of error (for means)
Coefficient of variation
Two-proportion z-test

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Two-proportion z-test".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "p̂c = (X₁ + X₂)/(n₁ + n₂); combined success rate across both samples, used in the test statistic under H₀"?

options.text: Pooled proportion
Median
Standard error of x̄
Stratified random sample

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Pooled proportion".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "(p̂₁ - p̂₂) ± z*·√(p̂₁(1-p̂₁)/n₁ + p̂₂(1-p̂₂)/n₂); uses unpooled standard error since we don't assume p₁ = p₂"?

options.text: Random assignment
CI for p₁ - p₂
Normal distribution
Placebo

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "CI for p₁ - p₂".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "n₁p̂c ≥ 10, n₁(1-p̂c) ≥ 10, n₂p̂c ≥ 10, and n₂(1-p̂c) ≥ 10 for the z-test"?

options.text: Independence requirement
Marginal distribution
Large Counts condition (two samples)
Association in two-way tables

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Large Counts condition (two samples)".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "Observations in one sample must not influence observations in the other; required for valid two-proportion inference"?

options.text: Margin of error
Population regression model
Z-score
Independent samples

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Independent samples".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following statements about Comparing Two Proportions is accurate?

options.text: The three principles of experimental design are control (using comparison groups), randomization (assigning treatments randomly to reduce bias), and replication (using enough subjects to detect real effects).
Comparing Two Proportions tests whether two population proportions differ using the test statistic z = (p̂₁ - p̂₂)/√(p̂c(1-p̂c)(1/n₁ + 1/n₂)), where p̂c is the pooled proportion under H₀: p₁ = p₂.
Paired designs are more powerful than independent two-sample designs because they eliminate subject-to-subject variability, isolating the treatment effect.
Conditional Probability measures the probability of an event given that another event has occurred: P(A|B) = P(A and B)/P(B).

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Comparing Two Proportions as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which statement correctly describes an aspect of Comparing Two Proportions?

options.text: Confidence Intervals for Means estimate an unknown population mean μ using x̄ ± t*(s/√n), where t* comes from the t-distribution with df = n-1.
Confidence intervals for p₁ - p₂ use the formula (p̂₁ - p̂₂) ± z*√(p̂₁(1-p̂₁)/n₁ + p̂₂(1-p̂₂)/n₂).
The width of the interval is determined by the confidence level (higher → wider), sample size (larger → narrower), and sample variability (larger s → wider).
The degrees of freedom are approximated by the Welch formula (or conservatively using the smaller of n₁-1 and n₂-1).

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Comparing Two Proportions as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which is true specifically about Comparing Two Proportions?

options.text: Technology output typically provides b₁, SE(b₁), t, and p-value directly, so interpreting computer regression output is an essential skill.
Residuals and Least-Squares Regression fit a linear model ŷ = a + bx to bivariate data by minimizing the sum of squared residuals (Σ(y - ŷ)²).
Comparing proportions is essential in clinical trials, A/B testing, and any study comparing success rates between groups.
Model comparison using r² and residual plots helps determine which model (linear, exponential, power) best fits the data.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Comparing Two Proportions as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best explains a key idea of Comparing Two Proportions?

options.text: Understanding this distinction is critical—many important questions (smoking's effect on health, educational interventions) rely on observational data when experiments are unethical or impractical.
For any random variables: E(X ± Y) = E(X) ± E(Y), always.
Standardization and Z-Scores transform raw data values into a common scale by expressing each observation as the number of standard deviations it falls above or below the mean: z = (x - μ)/σ.
Comparing Two Proportions tests whether two population proportions differ using the test statistic z = (p̂₁ - p̂₂)/√(p̂c(1-p̂c)(1/n₁ + 1/n₂)), where p̂c is the pooled proportion under H₀: p₁ = p₂.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Comparing Two Proportions as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which accurately reflects the content on Comparing Two Proportions?

options.text: Marginal distributions describe each variable alone (row or column totals divided by the grand total), while conditional distributions show the distribution of one variable within levels of the other.
Confidence Intervals for Means estimate an unknown population mean μ using x̄ ± t*(s/√n), where t* comes from the t-distribution with df = n-1.
Confidence intervals for p₁ - p₂ use the formula (p̂₁ - p̂₂) ± z*√(p̂₁(1-p̂₁)/n₁ + p̂₂(1-p̂₂)/n₂).
Paired designs are more powerful than independent two-sample designs because they eliminate subject-to-subject variability, isolating the treatment effect.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Comparing Two Proportions as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following is a real-world application of Comparing Two Proportions?

options.text: Analyzing the relationship between study hours and exam scores to determine what proportion (r²) of score variation is explained by study time
Clinical trials comparing cure rates between drug and placebo groups to determine whether the drug is more effective than no treatment
Hospital administrators constructing a 95% confidence interval for mean patient wait time to evaluate whether current staffing levels meet service standards
Using boxplots and five-number summaries to compare test score distributions across multiple schools or demographic groups

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Comparing Two Proportions.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which example demonstrates the practical relevance of Comparing Two Proportions?

options.text: Tire wear studies using matched-pairs design: placing competing tire brands on opposite sides of the same car to control for driving conditions
Online survey nonresponse bias where only highly motivated or opinionated individuals choose to respond, skewing results toward extreme views
Political campaigns comparing voter support proportions between two demographic groups to target messaging strategies
Public health officials testing whether the proportion of residents with elevated lead levels differs from the national rate to justify environmental remediation

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Comparing Two Proportions.

quiz_questions.question_text: How is Comparing Two Proportions applied in real-world contexts?

options.text: Public health surveys constructing confidence intervals for vaccination rates in a community to guide immunization campaign decisions
Education research comparing graduation rates between students enrolled in a mentoring program vs. those who were not
Linearizing the relationship between time and radioactive decay to estimate half-lives using transformed regression
Hospital administrators constructing a 95% confidence interval for mean patient wait time to evaluate whether current staffing levels meet service standards

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Comparing Two Proportions.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which scenario illustrates the principles of Comparing Two Proportions?

options.text: Clinical trials comparing cure rates between drug and placebo groups to determine whether the drug is more effective than no treatment
Pharmaceutical clinical trials using double-blind, placebo-controlled, randomized designs to test new drug efficacy while meeting FDA requirements
Testing whether a coin is fair (H₀: p = 0.5) by flipping it 100 times and computing the z-statistic from the observed proportion of heads
Identifying confounding variables in public health studies, such as separating the effect of diet from exercise on heart disease rates

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Comparing Two Proportions.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best shows Comparing Two Proportions in practice?

options.text: Testing whether advertising spending significantly predicts sales revenue (H₀: β₁ = 0) to justify continued marketing budget allocation
Demonstrating the CLT with simulations: rolling dice (uniform distribution) and plotting the mean of 30 rolls—the distribution of means is approximately normal despite the uniform population
Taste tests where each consumer rates two products, analyzing the paired differences to determine if there is a preference for one product over the other
Political campaigns comparing voter support proportions between two demographic groups to target messaging strategies

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Comparing Two Proportions.
